scientificName: Synodontis petricola — CAUTION: virtually all fish sold under this name in the aquarium trade are actually S. lucipinnis (Dwarf Petricola, fw-fish-327), a smaller species formally described in 2006. True S. petricola reaches ~14 cm and is rarely exported; confirm ID before purchase.
alsoKnownAs: Cuckoo Catfish (shared trade name — also misapplied to S. multipunctatus)

nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral and sub-littoral zones of Lake Tanganyika; shelters in crevices and caves among tumbled boulders and cobble at shallow to mid depths, typically 5–40 m

decorPreferences: numerous caves and crevices formed by stacked flat rocks or slate
smooth rounded boulders and cobble mimicking Tanganyikan rocky littoral
tight rock piles with interconnecting tunnels large enough for adult fish
sandy or fine-gravel substrate between rock structures
avoid sharp substrate edges that may abrade the fleshy barbels
minimal or no planting required; Java fern or Anubias on rock tolerated

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of sinking prepared and frozen foods: high-quality catfish pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworm, frozen brine shrimp, mysis, and daphnia. Wild fish are aufwuchs scrapers and opportunistic scavengers on rocky substrates, consuming biofilm, algae, invertebrates, and detritus. Feed after lights-out to ensure this nocturnal species competes for food before diurnal tankmates. Supplement diet with vegetable matter (blanched courgette, spinach) and vitamin-enriched frozen foods to support long-term health.

goodWith: Tanganyikan cichlids of robust size (Tropheus, Petrochromis, Cyphotilapia frontosa)
other Synodontis species of similar or larger size
shell-dwellers with adequate rock caves nearby
avoidWith: small fish or fry (opportunistic predation — larger than S. lucipinnis, greater risk)
fish requiring soft or acidic water (incompatible chemistry)
soft-water tetras, rasboras, or South American cichlids
very small shell-dweller cichlids that may be harassed or crowded out of caves

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen photo for Synodontis petricola; verify it shows true S. petricola rather than S. lucipinnis before use. Check image tag on FishBase species page.

summary: The true Petricola Catfish from Lake Tanganyika — larger (~14 cm), rarely traded, and almost always misidentified in shops as S. lucipinnis. Requires hard alkaline Tanganyikan water (pH 8.0–9.0), scaleless so copper-lethal; keep in groups of 3+ with ample rock cover.
careNotes:
SPECIES IDENTITY WARNING: Synodontis petricola is the original "Petricola Catfish" described by Mathes in 1959, but since 2006 it is known that the vast majority of fish sold under this name in the aquarium trade are actually Synodontis lucipinnis (Wright & Bailey, 2006) — see fw-fish-327. S. lucipinnis was previously lumped with S. petricola before formal morphometric separation; it remains the dominant trade fish because it is smaller, more prolific in export collections, and superficially identical in colour pattern. True S. petricola grows to approximately 14 cm (vs. ~9 cm for S. lucipinnis) and is only sporadically available from specialist Lake Tanganyika importers. If a fish labelled "petricola" is under 10 cm at apparent adult size, it is almost certainly S. lucipinnis.

Care requirements for true S. petricola are essentially identical to S. lucipinnis but scaled up for a larger fish. A 200 L tank (minimum 120 cm footprint) is recommended to house a group of three adults with adequate territorial caves. Lake Tanganyika water chemistry is non-negotiable: pH 8.0–9.0, GH 10–20°H, KH 10–18°H, temperature 23–27°C. Soft or acidic water leads to chronic stress, colour loss, and shortened lifespan.

As a scaleless catfish, S. petricola is highly sensitive to copper-based medications — do not dose any copper treatment in a tank housing this species, even at low concentrations. Use formalin-based or salt-based alternatives when treating disease.

The species is strictly nocturnal; offer sinking foods after lights-out to prevent cichlid tankmates from intercepting all food. Décor should emphasise multiple rock caves and crevices of sufficient size for adult fish (ca. 5–6 cm opening). Keep in groups of at least 3; solitary specimens become withdrawn and may refuse food. Maintain strong oxygenation consistent with the deep, oligotrophic nature of Lake Tanganyika.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported but is uncommon and less documented than for S. lucipinnis. S. petricola is understood to be an egg-scatterer depositing eggs among rock crevices; no parental care is given and adults will consume eggs if not removed. The species reportedly exploits mouthbrooding cichlids in the wild — depositing eggs near spawning cichlids so that fry are inadvertently incubated by the cichlid host (cuckoo parasitism), a strategy shared with S. multipunctatus. Replicating this in captivity requires pairing with a Tanganyikan mouthbrooder (e.g., Tropheus). Fry require a separate rearing tank with infusoria or finely powdered fry food, transitioning to micro-pellets at 2–3 weeks. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 18–24 months. Due to rarity and inconsistent captive-breeding reports, breedingDifficulty is rated "difficult".

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis petricola
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to ~14 cm), temperature 23–27°C, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness 10–20°H, native range (Lake Tanganyika), habitat (rocky littoral), diet, and trade-confusion note distinguishing S. petricola from S. lucipinnis. Confirmed rarity in trade and cuckoo-parasitism breeding strategy.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis petricola
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 14 cm TL), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), original description (Mathes 1959), native range (Lake Tanganyika basin: DRC, Tanzania, Zambia, Burundi), temperature tolerance, and pH range. Confirmed depth range and rocky habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Synodontis care overview; confirmed omnivorous sinking-food diet, group-keeping recommendation, nocturnal feeding habit, and copper sensitivity for scaleless catfish. Supplemented hobbyist guidance on tank size and Tanganyika water parameters.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis petricola species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed species-confusion history with S. lucipinnis, adult size ~14 cm for true S. petricola vs ~9 cm for S. lucipinnis, Tanganyikan hard-alkaline water requirements (pH 8.0–9.0), nocturnal feeding, group-keeping of 3+ individuals, and cuckoo parasitism breeding note.

additional.name: Wright, J.J. & Bailey, R.M. (2006) — Systematic revision distinguishing S. lucipinnis from S. petricola, via FishBase reference
additional.notes: Foundational taxonomic reference confirming formal separation of S. lucipinnis from S. petricola in 2006 based on morphometric and meristic characters. Establishes that most trade "petricola" specimens are S. lucipinnis, and that true S. petricola is the larger of the two species (up to 14 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with reported cuckoo-parasitism of mouthbrooding cichlids in the wild (eggs deposited near spawning cichlids for accidental oral incubation). Captive breeding uncommon; eggs deposited in rock crevices, adults consume eggs if not removed. Fry need separate rearing with micro-foods. Maturity at ~18–24 months.
